hub_subtitle: Conversação para Viagens, Encontros Reais e Cultura

lesson.menu_title: The Story of Me -- Your Past, Your Languages, Your Life
lesson.menu_desc: Telling your life story: past simple vs present perfect, and the words of a life abroad -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- The Story of Me
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 2 -- The Story of Me

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it's Astrid, from the bar in Reykjavik. I really enjoyed our conversation the other night. I don't often meet someone who has traveled as much as you have. I've been thinking about what you said, that you don't study a language, you live it. It really stuck with me. A group of us are meeting in Bergen next month to practice our English over dinner, and I would love for you to join, even by video call. You picked up French by living it, so you'll fit right in. Let me know. Take care, and keep cycling.

Welcome back to the show. Today, a question I get all the time: what is the best way to learn a language as an adult? Here is the honest answer. The people who succeed are almost never the ones with the best memory. They are the ones who stick with it. They make mistakes, they feel silly, and they keep going anyway. Research shows that adults who immerse themselves in real conversation, even for twenty minutes a day, improve far faster than those who only study grammar rules. So my advice is simple: stop trying to be perfect, and start trying to communicate. Find real people, have real conversations, and let the language grow. That is how fluency really happens.

vocab.title: Match each expression to its meaning
defs: to surround yourself completely with something
to go and live in another country
to learn something informally, by doing or using it
to feel accepted as part of a group
to stop doing something before it is finished
to look or seem like everyone around you
to get used to a new place and feel at home

paras: Marcelo has always had a gift for languages. Back in the 1980s, he started studying English at school, but life kept interrupting it. He took courses, he traveled, and then, again and again, he gave it up -- never for long. Over the years, he has come back to English more times than he can count.

The real turning point was French. In his thirties, Marcelo joined a French company and worked there for eighteen years. He did not sit in a classroom; he immersed himself in the language, spoke it every day, and eventually became fluent. 'I picked it up by living it,' he says. 'That is how I learned French, and that is how I want to learn English.'

Today, Marcelo has not given up on English -- far from it. He has listened to podcasts every morning for years, and right now he is reading two English books at the same time. He has never lost the passion; he has simply been waiting for the right structure. In November, he is cycling through Norway and Iceland, and this time he wants to talk to everyone he meets.
reading.source: Adapted from a language-learning feature, The Polyglot Life

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Marcelo failed at English because he gave it up in the 1980s and never tried again.
Marcelo has never given up on languages, and he learns them by living them, not in a classroom.
Marcelo only speaks French and has no interest in learning English.

items: Marcelo learned French by studying hard in a classroom.
The text says he did not sit in a classroom -- he immersed himself and picked it up by living it.
Over the years, Marcelo has come back to English many times.
The first paragraph says he has come back to English more times than he can count.
Marcelo gave up on English completely and never returned to it.
The text says he has not given up -- he has listened to podcasts for years and is reading two books now.
Right now, Marcelo is reading two English books at the same time.
The third paragraph states this directly.

gapfill.text: Notice: we use the past simple for finished actions at a finished time -- 'I started in the 1980s', 'I joined a company', 'I picked up French'. We use the present perfect for experience and duration up to now -- 'I have listened for years', 'I have come back again and again'. Signal words: ago, in 2005, back in the 80s point to the past simple; for, since, over the years, never point to the present perfect.

bank.label: Useful language for telling your language story
items: I have never given up on ...
I gave it up, but I came back to it.
I don't study a language -- I live it.

lf.title: Past simple or present perfect?
items: French when I worked in France.

lf.text: Notice: sentences A and C describe finished moments, so they use the past simple (picked up, started). Sentences B and D describe experience and duration up to now, so they use the present perfect (have listened, have never given up). The time words tell you which one to choose: 'when I worked', 'back in the 1980s' point to the past; 'for years', 'never' point to the present perfect.

items: A traveler asks how many languages you speak. Answer with depth: name them, and say when and how you learned each one (past simple).
Tell the story of your English: when you started, the times you gave it up, and how you have always come back (past simple + present perfect).
Describe how you learned French: joining a French company, immersing yourself, picking it up over eighteen years. Use time expressions: back in, for, over the years.
Talk about your life and travels: where you have been, what you have never done, and one trip you will never forget.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = started, 2 = joined, 3 = picked up, 4 = immersed, 5 = has listened to, 6 = has come back.
Model (past simple): 'I started English at school back in the 1980s, and I learned French when I worked in France for eighteen years.'
Model (present perfect): 'I have never given up on English. I have listened to podcasts for years, and I have come back to it again and again.'
Past vs present perfect: use the past simple for finished actions at a finished time; use the present perfect for experience and duration up to now (for / since).
answerkey.note: With 'ago' always use the past simple: 'I moved abroad twenty years ago.' Say 'I have lived here for ten years' (still true) but 'I lived there in 2005' (finished).
